From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Section of plants in the genus Populus

| *Populus sect. Aigeiros* | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| *Populus fremontii* in fall color | |
| [Scientific classification](/wiki/Taxonomy_(biology) "Taxonomy (biology)") [Edit this classification](/wiki/Template:Taxonomy/Populus_sect._Aigeiros "Edit this classification") | |
| Kingdom: | [Plantae](/wiki/Plant "Plant") |
| *Clade*: | [Tracheophytes](/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant") |
| *Clade*: | [Angiosperms](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") |
| *Clade*: | [Eudicots](/wiki/Eudicots "Eudicots") |
| *Clade*: | [Rosids](/wiki/Rosids "Rosids") |
| Order: | [Malpighiales](/wiki/Malpighiales "Malpighiales") |
| Family: | [Salicaceae](/wiki/Salicaceae "Salicaceae") |
| Genus: | [*Populus*](/wiki/Populus "Populus") |
| Section: | *Populus* sect. *Aigeiros* [Duby](/wiki/Jean_%C3%89tienne_Duby "Jean Étienne Duby")[[1]](#cite_note-1) |
| [Type species](/wiki/Type_species "Type species") | |
| *[Populus nigra](/wiki/Populus_nigra "Populus nigra")*  L. | |
| Species | |
| * *[Populus deltoides](/wiki/Populus_deltoides "Populus deltoides")* [L.](/wiki/Carl_Linnaeus "Carl Linnaeus") * *[Populus fremontii](/wiki/Populus_fremontii "Populus fremontii")* * *[Populus nigra](/wiki/Populus_nigra "Populus nigra")* [L.](/wiki/Carl_Linnaeus "Carl Linnaeus") | |

***Populus* section *Aigeiros*** is a [section](/wiki/Section_(botany) "Section (botany)") of three species in the genus *[Populus](/wiki/Populus "Populus")*, the poplars. Like some other species in the genus *Populus*, they are commonly known as [cottonwoods](/wiki/Cottonwood_(disambiguation) "Cottonwood (disambiguation)"). The species are native to North America, Europe, and western Asia. In the past, as many as six species were recognized, but recent trends have been to accept just three species, treating the others as subspecies of *P. deltoides*.

They are large, deciduous trees that are 15–30 m (50–100 ft) tall and diameters of 4 m (13 ft),[[2]](#cite_note-2) distinguished by thick, deeply fissured bark and triangular-based to diamond-shaped leaves that are green on both sides (without the whitish wax on the undersides) and without any obvious [balsam](/wiki/Balsam "Balsam") scent in spring. An important feature of the leaves is the [petiole](/wiki/Petiole_(botany) "Petiole (botany)"), which is flattened sideways so that the leaves have a particular type of movement in the wind.

Male and female flowers are in separate [catkins](/wiki/Catkin "Catkin"), appearing before the leaves in spring. The seeds are borne on cottony structures that allow them to be blown long distances in the air before settling to ground.

The cottonwoods are exceptionally tolerant of flooding, erosion, and flood deposits filling around the trunk.

Although each of the three cottonwood species has a different leaf pattern, they all have the same general diamond leaf shape.

## Species

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Populus_sect._Aigeiros&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Species")]

### *Populus deltoides*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Populus_sect._Aigeiros&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Populus deltoides")]

[Eastern cottonwood](/wiki/Populus_deltoides "Populus deltoides") (*Populus deltoides*) is one of the largest North American [hardwood](/wiki/Hardwood "Hardwood") trees, although the wood is rather soft. It is a [riparian zone](/wiki/Riparian_zone "Riparian zone") tree. It occurs throughout the eastern United States, parts of southern Canada, and northern Mexico. The [leaves](/wiki/Leaf "Leaf") are alternate and simple, with coarsely toothed (crenate/serrate) edges, and subcordate at the base. The leaf shape is roughly triangular, hence the species name, *deltoides*. Their winter buds are enrobed in a protective, fragrant resin that coats young leaves when they unfurl from the bud.

In the typical subspecies *P. d. deltoides* ([Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") south to northern [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") and west to about [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan")), the leaves are broad and triangular, 7–15 cm across at the base. Further west ([Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") south to eastern [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")), the subspecies *P. d. monilifera* (plains cottonwood; syn. *P. sargentii*) has somewhat narrower leaves, 5–10 cm wide at the base. This is also the [state tree](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_trees "List of U.S. state trees") of [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"), and [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"). In West Texas, [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), and [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), the subspecies *P. d. wislizeni* (Rio Grande cottonwood; syn. *P. wislizeni*) occurs.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Cottonwood_20090521_083459_1.jpg/250px-Cottonwood_20090521_083459_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cottonwood_20090521_083459_1.jpg)

Seeds and seed hairs from an eastern cottonwood

### *Populus fremontii*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Populus_sect._Aigeiros&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Populus fremontii")]

[Fremont's cottonwood](/wiki/Populus_fremontii "Populus fremontii") (*P. fremontii*) is native to the southwestern United States and western Mexico.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In the United States, the species can be found in [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"), [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), and [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"). In Mexico, it can be found in the states of [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California"), [Baja California Sur](/wiki/Baja_California_Sur "Baja California Sur"), [Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora"), [Chihuahua](/wiki/Chihuahua_(state) "Chihuahua (state)"), [Coahuila](/wiki/Coahuila "Coahuila"), [Nuevo León](/wiki/Nuevo_Le%C3%B3n "Nuevo León"), [Mexico State](/wiki/Mexico_State "Mexico State"), and [Puebla](/wiki/Puebla "Puebla").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) It differs from the eastern cottonwood mainly in the leaves having fewer, larger serrations on the edge, and small differences in the flower and seed pod structure. Some taxonomists formerly considered *P. fremontii* to be a subspecies of *P. deltoides*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### *Populus nigra*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Populus_sect._Aigeiros&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Populus nigra")]

[Black poplar](/wiki/Populus_nigra "Populus nigra") (*P. nigra*) is native to Europe and Western Asia, is distinct in its much smaller leaves, 5–11 cm (2.0–4.3 in) across, with a more rhombic (diamond) shape.

## Cultivation and uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Populus_sect._Aigeiros&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Cultivation and uses")]

Cottonwoods are widely grown for [timber](/wiki/Timber "Timber") production along wet river banks, where their exceptional growth rate provides a large crop of wood within just 10–30 years. The wood is coarse and of fairly low value, used for pallet boxes, shipping crates, and similar purposes where a cheap but strong enough wood is suitable. They are also widely grown as screens and [shelterbelts](/wiki/Shelterbelt "Shelterbelt"). Many of the cottonwoods grown commercially are the [hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_(biology) "Hybrid (biology)") of eastern cottonwood and black poplar, *Populus × canadensis* (hybrid black poplar or Carolina poplar).

Cottonwood bark is often a favorite medium for artisans. The bark, which is usually harvested in the fall after a tree's death, is generally very soft and easy to carve.

Cottonwood is one of the poorest [wood fuels](/wiki/Wood_fuel "Wood fuel"); it does not dry well, and rots quickly. It splits poorly, because it is very fibrous. It produces a low level of energy per unit of volume of wood.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Cottonwoods serve as food for the [caterpillars](/wiki/Caterpillar "Caterpillar") of several [Lepidoptera](/wiki/Lepidoptera "Lepidoptera").

This tree is 106 feet (32.31 meters) tall.
